But here is the hard truth most operators are ignoring: raw code is now a commodity. When anyone can spin up a fully compiled front-end in seconds using models like GLM 5.2, speed ceases to be a competitive advantage. The differentiator is no longer the model itself—it is the system architecture built around it. Deploying out-of-the-box automation without rigorous data engineering is a fast track to three critical failure states: 1. Reward-Hacking: Native optimization loops breaking down under complex corporate edge cases. 2. Token Overhead: Unoptimized prompt pipelines causing massive, unexpected compute bills. 3. Data Leakage: Standard API setups compromising enterprise-grade data isolation. True leverage doesn’t come from rushing a basic template to deployment. It comes from custom infrastructure, strict compliance control, and engineering restraint. We don't build generic wrappers. I was not one to fall for ChatGPT&39;s BS when it just gives me what I want to hear or simply toss guesses or assumptions at me which are dead wrong. Sometimes AI hits the nail on the head, but all too often it&39;s full of crap. I&39;ve even tested it to see if it even knew how to do some simple research on something I was well versed in to see if it either knew or could look up the correct answer regarding some old technology; it got it wrong. I don&39;t care that the personality tells me that I was right to call it out, but I&39;m really unhappy at how damn often it gets shit wrong. For every one question it answers correctly, it will respond with incorrect information the next 10. What&39;s worse is that after it hands me something to try, and it doesn&39;t work, after 3 different responses or so, it goes back to the first suggestion as if it forgot that it suggested it and that it did not work. Not only does AI seem to have a really bad time at also retaining things, but since it&39;s a "learning" model, it can just as easily unlearn as quickly as it learns meaning that it can never be trusted in what it THINKS it knows. I&39;ve yelled at ChatGPT for being so wrong so often and sometimes I don&39;t even have to check what it suggests to me to know it handed me something useless. In the grand scheme of things, AI is junk! Right now I&39;m really angry because of the AI slop shell scripts that I have to review that people could easily study what is already available (like a 30 line shell script) instead of jumping straight to AI which will hand them a messy, nonsensical, difficult to look over 600 line shell script (I&39;m not exaggerating the 600 line messy shell script crapped out by AI part, either).

☸️ Most ML engineers avoid Kubernetes because it looks intimidating. But avoiding it doesn't make it go away, it just makes you dependent on someone else who understands it. Here's Kubernetes for ML in 5 minutes 👇 Docker runs one container. Kubernetes runs hundreds of containers reliably, with scaling, self-healing, and automation built in. Why ML workloads need K8s: ⚡ GPU-intensive → Resource requests & limits prevent resource starvation 📈 Traffic spikes → Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales automatically 💾 Persistent storage → Volumes survive pod restarts ⏰ Scheduled retraining → CronJobs automate recurring workflows The 4 Kubernetes objects that cover most ML deployments: • Pod → Runs your container • Deployment → Manages replicas & updates • Service → Stable endpoint for your app • Ingress → Routes external traffic Flow: Ingress → Service → Deployment → Pods One lesson most tutorials skip: Always define resource requests and limits. Without them: ❌ Autoscaling becomes unreliable ❌ One workload can consume an entire node ❌ Performance becomes unpredictable And then there's Helm. Instead of managing dozens of YAML files, Helm lets you template once and deploy consistently across environments. You don't need to master Kubernetes overnight. Start with managed platforms like GKE, EKS, or AKS and focus on your ML workloads first. The workflow ran flawlessly for 47 days. 14,000+ successful executions. Zero issues. On day 48, at 3:42 AM, a payment gateway returned a 503 instead of a 200. Without error handling: the entire pipeline crashed silently. No Slack alert. No retry. No fallback. Nobody noticed for 72 hours. 4,200 customer invoices were never processed. The team found out from a customer complaint — not from their own monitoring. With error handling: the node retried 3 times with exponential backoff (2s, 4s, 8s). On the third failure, it triggered a fallback path that routed to a backup payment processor. Simultaneously, a Slack alert hit the on-call channel in 12 seconds. Failed records were queued in a dead letter queue for manual review. Total customer impact: zero. 5 error handling patterns every production workflow needs: 1. Retry with exponential backoff — 73% of transient API failures self-resolve within 3 attempts 2. Fallback/alternative paths — if the primary service is down, route to a backup 3. Dead letter queues — failed items go somewhere recoverable, not into the void 4. Real-time alert notifications — Slack, PagerDuty, email. Within seconds, not days 5. Graceful degradation — processing 9,800 of 10,000 records is infinitely better than processing zero Autonoly has all 5 built into the workflow builder. Every node supports retry logic, error branching, and alert triggers. No custom code required. Stop building for the happy path. Build for day 48. Your dashboard's 7-day average has been a running total this whole time. Write avg(sales) OVER (ORDER BY day) with no frame and Postgres defaults to RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING — it averages every row from the start, not a sliding window. Your "moving average" slowly flattens into a cumulative total and quietly understates every recent trend. The fix is one clause: ROWS BETWEEN 6 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW pins a fixed 7-row window for a true moving average. Same trap applies to SUM, MIN and MAX — always state the frame. Save this for the next time a chart looks "smoothed" for no reason. Follow for daily SQL & dev one-liners. sql postgres postgresql windowfunctions database dataengineering sqltips coding

A proposed bill reportedly introduced by Senator Bernie Sanders has sparked discussion about how the benefits of artificial intelligence should be shared across society. The proposal centers on the idea that as AI companies generate massive economic value through automation and productivity gains, a portion of that wealth could be returned directly to the public. Supporters argue that AI is built on decades of publicly funded research, infrastructure, and data generated by society, making it reasonable for citizens to share in the financial upside. They see such proposals as a potential way to address growing concerns about wealth concentration and job displacement. Critics, however, question the practicality of government-mandated ownership stakes, warning that such measures could discourage investment, innovation, and competition in a rapidly evolving industry. Others argue that alternative approaches, such as taxation or universal dividend programs, may be more feasible. The broader debate reflects a key question of the AI era: who should benefit from the enormous value created by advanced artificial intelligence, and how should those benefits be distributed? . . . . startup ai automation tech entrepreneurship

𝗪𝗵𝘆 𝗜 𝗗𝗼𝗻’𝘁 𝗔𝗴𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗪𝗶𝘁𝗵 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗛𝗮𝘁𝗲 𝗔𝗿𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱 "𝗩𝗶𝗯𝗲 𝗖𝗼𝗱𝗶𝗻𝗴" Lately, I've seen a lot of developers criticizing "vibe coding" and AI-assisted development as if it's somehow destroying software engineering. I see it differently. The problem isn't the tools. The problem is how people use them. A calculator didn't kill mathematics. GitHub didn't kill programming. Frameworks didn't kill software engineering. AI won't either. What AI does is amplify the capabilities of the person using it. If someone lacks understanding of architecture, databases, security, scalability, debugging, or software design principles, AI will simply help them make mistakes faster. But for experienced developers who understand the fundamentals, AI becomes a force multiplier. Over the last few months, I've shipped hundreds of projects ranging from SaaS platforms, digital products, business applications, web portals, mobile applications, dashboards, and desktop software. AI-assisted development didn't replace my expertise it accelerated execution. Instead of spending hours on repetitive boilerplate code, I spend more time solving business problems, improving user experiences, refining architecture, and delivering products faster. The reality is that clients don't pay for how many lines of code you write. They pay for solutions. They pay for products that work. They pay for results. The developers who will thrive in the coming years won't be the ones fighting AI. They'll be the ones learning how to combine technical expertise, product thinking, and AI tools to build better software faster than ever before. AI is not replacing developers. Developers who know how to leverage AI effectively are replacing those who refuse to adapt. What's your take on AI-assisted development and vibe coding? Most IAM reviews focus on individual permissions. But attackers don't think that way. They look for combinations. A permission that appears harmless on its own can become dangerous when combined with another permission elsewhere in the environment. Consider examples like: • KMS Decrypt + S3 Read • Secrets Manager Access + Lambda Update • CloudTrail Stop Logging + IAM Modification • S3 Policy Changes + Bucket Read/Write Individually, these permissions may not trigger major concern. Together, they can create a direct path to data exposure, privilege escalation, or reduced visibility during an attack. This is one of the biggest challenges in cloud security today. Most tools identify individual findings. Few analyze how permissions interact across identities, resources, and services to create hidden attack paths. Securitain was built to address exactly this problem. By analyzing IAM permissions, trust relationships, resource access, and toxic permission combinations, Securitain helps AWS teams uncover risks that may remain hidden in traditional security reviews. Because the real risk is often not a single permission. It's the combination. 🔐 Detect hidden attack paths 🔐 Identify toxic permission combinations 🔐 Prioritize risks based on real impact 🔐 Improve AWS IAM security posture Securitain by Cloudain Making cloud security findings easier to understand, prioritize, and remediate.
